name: Cyclopes Extended
description: The Cyclopes, in Greek mythology, are a primordial race of giants, each possessing a single eye in the middle of their forehead. They are often depicted as brutish and uncivilized, though some accounts attribute great skill and craftsmanship to them. Hesiod's Theogony describes them as the sons of Gaia and Uranus, imprisoned by their father, and later freed by Zeus to aid him in the Titanomachy. These Cyclopes, named Arges, Steropes, and Brontes, were renowned for their strength and skill in forging, crafting Zeus' thunderbolts, Hades' helmet of invisibility, and Poseidon's trident. Homer's Odyssey features a different group of Cyclopes, a lawless and savage tribe of shepherds living in caves, most famously represented by Polyphemus, son of Poseidon, who is blinded by Odysseus. These Cyclopes are not depicted as craftsmen but as monstrous cannibals. The term 'Cyclops' can refer to either type, and the exact relationship between the two groups remains ambiguous in mythology. Their immense size and singular eye contribute to their fearsome reputation, solidifying their place as iconic figures in Greek lore. Beyond forging and brute strength, some accounts hint at other unique abilities linked to their divine parentage or the nature of their single eye, such as limited precognition or enhanced senses.
subtitle: One-eyed giants of Greek myth
appearance: The Cyclopes are generally depicted as enormous, towering figures. Their most distinctive feature is the single, large eye located in the center of their forehead. The skin color varies depending on the source, but it is often described as rough and weathered. The Cyclopes of the Theogony are likely more humanoid, while Polyphemus and his brethren are often portrayed with animalistic features, such as thick hair and coarse skin. They are frequently shown wielding crude weapons, reflecting their uncivilized nature, or in the case of the craftsmen, hammers and forging tools. Their size is consistently emphasized, reinforcing their intimidating presence.
habitat: The Cyclopes of the Theogony were imprisoned within the earth, later released to work in subterranean forges. Polyphemus and his tribe inhabited caves on a remote island, engaging in pastoral activities.
behavior: The behavior of the Cyclopes varies greatly depending on the specific type. The Cyclopes of Hesiod are skilled craftsmen, loyal to Zeus, and integral to the Olympian victory. Polyphemus and his tribe, on the other hand, are savage, lawless, and cannibalistic, exhibiting a complete lack of hospitality. Regardless of their specific occupation, Cyclopes are generally portrayed as solitary creatures, preferring their own company and lacking the social graces of humans or other mythological beings.

keyMyths.title: The Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: The Cyclopes, freed by Zeus, forged his thunderbolts, aiding the Olympians in their war against the Titans. Their craftsmanship proved crucial to the Olympian victory.

keyMyths.title: Odysseus and Polyphemus
keyMyths.description: Odysseus and his crew were trapped in the cave of Polyphemus, who devoured several of Odysseus' men. Odysseus blinded Polyphemus and escaped, incurring the wrath of Poseidon.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Describes the Cyclopes as sons of Gaia and Uranus, and their role in the Titanomachy.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Features the encounter between Odysseus and the Cyclops Polyphemus.
